SPE-221022-MS
Leveraging Large Language Models for Cost Management and Supply Chain
Optimization
Maryam Shahini, Carina Yu Wang, Marc A. Roeder, Sanjay Pethe, Sarah W. Coffman, Preston Howard, Bo Hu,
Hannah N. Wasserman, and Joshua Arnwine, Conocophillips
Copyright 2024, Society of Petroleum Engineers DOI 10.2118/221022-MS
This paper was prepared for presentation at the SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition held in New Orleans, Louisiana, USA, 23 - 25 September 2024.
This paper was selected for presentation by an SPE program committee following review of information contained in an abstract submitted by the author(s). Contents
of the paper have not been reviewed by the Society of Petroleum Engineers and are subject to correction by the author(s). The material does not necessarily reflect

Abstract
In this paper, we introduce a cutting-edge solution to the complex challenge of managing operational costs in
unconventional asset development, particularly concerning continuous well drilling, completion, and field
maintenance operations within the oil and gas sector. These complicated field operations involve hundreds
of service providers and incur vast volumes of invoices every year. Tremendous values are lost due to missed
opportunities to improve contracting strategy, optimize material and equipment supplies, and identify cost-
prohibitive design elements during drilling, completion, production, and plant maintenance through proper
spend categorization.
Leveraging the power of a machine learning solution, Large Language Model (LLM), and an interactive
user interface, we automate the challenging task of categorizing millions of invoices from diverse service
providers. Techniques including sentence transformation embedding, transfer learning, fine-tuning, and re-
training process are employed to enhance model performance and adaptability to diverse invoice types.
Through rigorous model training and iterative refinement facilitated by the user interface, our approach
attains an impressive accuracy exceeding 90% across all regions. Results prove that the Large Language
Model has a wide application in business optimization during unconventional asset development. This
automated algorithm provides real-time insights through spending and significantly reduces the turnaround
time. This study also enables direct identification of cost-saving opportunities such as potential reductions
in fuel expenses related to drilling and completion activities. The advanced analytics capabilities following
the modeling effort allow engineers and analysts in multiple functions to identify cost-saving opportunities
through customizing visualization tools in various areas.
This paper presents original methodology to adopt the Artificial Intelligence, i.e., Large Language Model,
in the oil and gas industry and demonstrate its values with case histories.
Keywords: Invoice Categorization, Artificial Intelligence, Supply Chain Optimization, Machine
Learning, Embedding, Large Language Model, Technological Innovation, Predictive Analytics

the advanced analytics that allow us to extract meaningful insights from complex datasets.
Data Sources and Characteristics
The primary data source for this study consists of tens of millions of invoice records generated from
operational activities in unconventional oil and gas asset development. These records originate from
multiple service providers involved in various stages of asset development, including drilling, completion,
production, and maintenance. Each invoice contains detailed information such as service descriptions,
material descriptions, line spend, quantities, unit prices, and total spend amounts. Due to the collaborative
nature of oil and gas projects, these invoices are not only numerous but also varied in format, content, and
level of detail.
Challenges
Invoice data often contain inconsistencies and errors due to manual entry, varying data entry standards
among providers, and differences in billing practices across regions. These inconsistencies can include
mismatched service codes, typographical errors, and incomplete descriptions, all of which complicate the
task of categorizing and analyzing the data accurately.
The complexity of the data is a significant challenge in this context. The data set includes hundreds of
thousands to millions of invoice entries annually, contributed by various service providers across multiple
geographic regions. The large volume and heterogeneity of the data necessitate robust preprocessing and
data management strategies to ensure that the input fed into the Large Language Model is of high quality
and uniformity.
The other main challenge in this task is managing the classification across an extensive, over 1500,
distinct classes. This huge number of categories caused complexity due to the diversity of invoice types,
services, and materials. This challenge is associated with the skewed distribution of invoices across these
classes, as some classes have many invoices while others are rare. Therefore, it causes the other common
issue in the dataset, which is the imbalance nature of the data. Some categories, such as those related
to routine maintenance or standard equipment, are overrepresented, while others, particularly specialized
services or emergency repairs, are underrepresented. This imbalance can bias the model towards more
frequent categories, potentially hindering its performance on less common but critical spending types.
The scalability of data processing systems to handle such large datasets efficiently and the adaptability
of these systems to accommodate data from new sources or regions are ongoing challenges. Each region
may introduce unique invoicing formats or operational terminologies, requiring continuous adjustments to
the data processing and model training frameworks.
Data Cleaning
Data cleaning, an important part of any machine learning process, ensures that the model is trained on
high-quality, relevant data and that the model's learning process is not obstructed or misled by extraneous
noise. The initial step in our data cleaning process is to identify missing values and for critical features
providing appropriate infilling. Any other features with a missing value percentage above 60%, are removed
from the training data. Selection of the most relevant features for the model is a critical step. This process
involves analyzing the importance of various attributes as well as re-training those that significantly impact
the accuracy of the results.
Additionally, the reliability of invoice labels across different regions is also evaluated. Based on this
evaluation, regions with unreliable labeling and old regions with no new invoices are excluded from the
dataset. This selective exclusion helps maintain the quality and consistency of the training data, avoiding
the bias or noise that could degrade the model's performance.

SPE-221022-MS
We also developed a customized dictionary specific to our data that maps acronyms and abbreviations
to their full word equivalents. This expansion of shortened forms into full words enhances clarity and
uniformity across the text data, facilitating better understanding and processing by the models.
Data Partitioning
In this project, an effective data partitioning strategy is very important for training, validation and testing the
model under realistic conditions. This has been done based on the date each invoice was created, ensuring
that the model is trained on historical data, validated on recent data, and tested on the most current data to
reflect real word operational scenarios. Our data partitioning is designed to maximize learning by using three
years of historical data while ensuring robust validation and testing using six months of the most recent data.
By regularly updating the partitioning, newer historical data is included in the training process, ensuring
that the model has the most up to date information to provide accurate predictions for most recent data.
Model Building and Deployment
Transitioning from data preparation, our next critical phase involves the robust model construction and
strategic deployment of our machine learning model. This stage converts clean and structured data into
predictive insights that are essential for informed decision-making.
Embedding
In this project focused on optimizing invoice processing, we utilized an advanced transformer model for
Language embedding. The model is specifically chosen for its ability to understand and process complicated
details within extensive invoice texts. Each invoice is converted into a high-dimensional vector that
effectively captures its semantic essence, therefore facilitating more accurate and efficient categorization.
The architecture of the transformer is adept at managing the lengthy and detailed invoice descriptions typical
in our industry, where each entry might contain multiple lines of detailed service descriptions and contractual
terms.
By encoding texts into a high-dimensional vectors, embedding model ensures that each invoice is
represented by a dense feature set, enhancing the subsequent classification accuracy by the downstream
Llama model.
Model Fine-Tuning
To adjust the Llama (Touvron 2023) model's parameters specifically for the task of invoice categorization,
fine-tuning the model is crucial for maintaining its accuracy and adaptability over time. Leveraging Pytorch
(Paszke 2019), a flexible deep learning framework, we implemented a process to continuously refine the
model based on the latest available data.
The criteria for selecting the model include overall accuracy, macro accuracy, and percentage of correct
predictions for spend values. These metrics ensure that the model not only performs well across a broad
spectrum of invoice categories, but also enhances its ability to accurately label high value transactions,
which is critical due to significant financial implications.
To improve the efficiency of the training process, we leverage GPU computing capabilities. GPUs provide
substantial acceleration compared to CPU due to their parallel processing abilities, significantly reducing
training time from days to just hours.
Model Results
The fine-tuned and selected model demonstrated a significant capability in achieving an overall accuracy
of above 90% and macro accuracy of above 85% across more than 1500 classes, showing its effectiveness
in handling a complex and large-scale categorization task.

Dynamic Updates
A custom dashboard using Streamlit is created to track the model's performance metrics in real-time,
providing clear visibility into accuracy rates, processing times, and error rates. The dashboard provides
users with invoice predictions as well as corresponding confidence scores. This interface helps end users
perform quality control (QC) on model predictions. Supply Chain users can correct any misclassifications
directly within the dashboard to enhance the data quality for retraining.
Model Re-Training
The model is scheduled for periodic retraining to adapt to new trends or changes in the invoicing procedures.
The comprehensive retraining process is designed to incorporate new data and QC'ed labels, allowing the
model to learn from the enriched dataset for improving its accuracy. The retrained model is then redeployed
in the operational environment to reflect these improvements.
Conclusions
In this project, we faced the challenge of classifying over a thousand categories for Supply Chain
organization in a timely manner. We have implemented sophisticated techniques to manage class imbalance
and enhance the discriminative power of the model. By harnessing advanced AI techniques such as
embedding, LLM, and leveraging GPU technology for computational efficiency, we have established a
robust system capable of handling large-scale data with high accuracy. Our methodology of temporal data
partitioning reflects a nuanced understanding of operational dynamics, ensuring that the model is trained on
historical data while validated and tested against the most current data available. This approach mitigates
potential overfitting and prepares the model for real-world applications by closely simulating the operational
environment.
The strategic integration of a user-driven Streamlit dashboard has been instrumental in refining model
performance, enabling real-time validation and correction of predictions. This feedback loop not only
enhances the model's accuracy but also promotes a collaborative environment where machine learning and
human expertise synergize to improve decision-making processes.
This project sets a new benchmark for the application of AI in the oil and gas sector, demonstrating not
just the feasibility but also the extensive benefits of AI in streamlining complex operational processes. As
we move forward, continuous improvement through model retraining, and updates based on user feedback
and new data, will remain central to our approach, ensuring that our solutions remain at the cutting edge of
technology and industry needs. Ongoing efforts in innovation using AI are leading to more sophisticated,
efficient, and economically viable operational solutions within the industry.
